story:
I drove far to the east to a place I’d never seen, a village that grew where a city once hung in the sky. The old stories said that city had been chained in place by iron links as wide as bicycle tires. War broke those chains. The city fell decades ago, and the anchors left behind now framed a square with a strange “sculpture” at its center.

It was a woman, tucked tight as if bracing for impact, one hand flung up toward the sky, the other clutched around a necklace, fingers locked around the pendant. Even from far away it unsettled me because nothing seemed to hold her there. Up close, the piece hit harder. Her face was caught somewhere between fear and grief—eyes wide, cheeks slick with tears, the expression so raw it made my stomach turn. It felt like a grim snapshot of the last thing people saw as the city came down.

The clothes were as arresting as her face. A green skirt billowed, thin enough to see light through, as if wire kept it in the exact shape of wind. Every fold had been frozen mid-twirl, every thread placed with the same merciless care. The eyes were so detailed I could see tiny veins painted across them the longer I stared. The longer I stared, the less I wanted to.

I meant to look away. The whole thing felt like a freeze frame before a body hits the ground. Across the street, Jake waved. He was on snack duty for the next leg of our trip, terribly proud of his “walking oats.” I managed a wave back, then turned to a small placard near the base, hoping to learn the artist’s name.

The title read: The Last Second. No signature jumped out, so I kept reading the description. “This is one of the residents of the floating city when it fell. She was a lead chrono-scientist in the labs above. When the city was attacked and split open, she was among those who went over the edge. Around her neck was one of the lab’s final prototypes: a device that stops time for the wearer. She activated it at the last instant, just before she reached the ground.”

The plaque went on. Other scientists who survived recognized her and the device. Her time was stopped, not the world’s, which meant no one could move her or bleed off her speed. They ran the numbers. With the velocity she had and the little distance left, there was no way to slow her safely before impact. Touching her would do nothing until the device released; then all momentum would return at once.

Her name, it said, was Yellen Cloudwolf, the lead researcher on the project. No one knew how long she had set the locket for—an hour, a year, a lifetime. When it expires, time around her will catch up. She will finish the fall she started. Until then, she hangs here, a person mistaken for art, held forever in the last heartbeat before death, a memorial to a city that used to float.

Road trip ruined.
